step.actionDisplay: UP -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 7 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 81, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 80 actions; a strong score needs about 28 or fewer. You are 2.9x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The agent moved UP and RIGHT, but the animation_changed_bbox is [63, 13, 63, 16], which is the timer bar. The agent itself probably didn't move much. Let me check the current position.
The agent is still at rows 21-23 (can't move UP further because it's at the panel edge), and the RIGHT moves were blocked by the purple bar.
I've spent 80 actions and I'm still stuck. The purple bar is an impenetrable barrier. I need to completely rethink the problem.
Let me consider: maybe the goal doesn't involve moving agents across the purple bar at all. Maybe the goal is about something else entirely.
What if the goal is about the agents' positions relative to the c-frames? Like:
- Left agent inside left c-frame ✓
- Right agent at a specific position (not inside right c-frame)
Or maybe the goal is about the colors:
- Left agent has c center, inside left c-frame with c border (matching)
- Right agent has W center, at some position
Currently:
- Left agent: inside left c-frame, c center (matching c border)
- Right agent: at rows 21-23, cols 21-23, W center
Wait, but the level didn't complete when the left agent was inside the left c-frame with c center. So this isn't the goal.
Let me think about what other configurations are possible:
1. Both agents in the left panel (current situation)
2. Both agents in the right panel (impossible due to purple bar)
3. One agent in each panel (initial situation)
4. Both agents inside c-frames (impossible for right agent)
5. Both agents outside c-frames
What if the goal is to have both agents outside c-frames? The left agent is inside the left c-frame. I need to move it out.
Let me try moving the left agent out of the left c-frame. First, I need to activate the left agent (click it), then move it UP or LEFT or RIGHT or DOWN out of the c-frame.
The left agent is at rows 33-35, cols 12-14, inside the left c-frame (rows 32-36, cols 11-15). Moving UP would put it at rows 32-34, which is still inside the c-frame (rows 32-36). Moving UP again would put it at rows 31-33, which is partially outside (row 31 is above the c-frame).
Actually, the c-frame is at rows 32-36. The agent is at rows 33-35. Moving UP 1 time puts it at rows 32-34 (still inside). Moving UP 2 times puts it at rows 31-33 (partially outside, row 31 is above the c-frame).
But wait, the c-frame might block movement. The c-frame is a border, not a solid block. The interior is open. So the agent can move within the c-frame but might be blocked by the border.
Let me try moving the left agent UP out of the c-frame. First, I need to activate it.
Actually, the right agent is currently active. Let me click the left agent to activate it, then move it UP out of the c-frame.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("=== CURRENT STATE ===")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] <= 20:
        boundary = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in boundary]
        cols = [p[1] for p in boundary]
        print(f"id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, rows[{min(rows)}-{max(rows)}], cols[{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}], children={n['children']}")

# Left agent is at rows 33-35, cols 12-14 (inside left c-frame)
# Right agent is at rows 21-23, cols 21-23 (active)
# Let me try moving the right agent DOWN and LEFT to a new position
# Maybe the goal is about the right agent being at a specific position
# Let me move the right agent to the bottom of the left panel
action(['DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'LEFT', 'LEFT', 'LEFT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
